behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is handler-to-queue handoff plus auth check together: reject HTTP and suppress enqueue in one flow. Unit tests of HMAC compare alone would miss accidental forwarding. Rejected System: behavior text does not require real network/OS/subprocess; in-process HTTP harness + fake queue can reproduce the failure.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler in-process with a real raw-body signature verifier and a spy/fake queue writer. Send mismatched signature request, assert 401 and assert queue write was never called.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: This is an ordering contract across modules (HTTP handler and queue persistence API). A pure unit test on one function is too low; a controlled queue double that resolves only after write lets us verify ack ordering. Rejected System: no explicit requirement for real external queue/network; ordering bug is catchable in-process.
behaviors.test_description: Use in-process server + instrumented queue adapter where `enqueue()` completion is observable. Assert response is not sent until enqueue promise resolves, and 200 is emitted only after write completion signal.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Behavior validates decoupling between request path and async processor path, which is module orchestration/state timing. Unit is too low because the failure appears at handoff boundary. Rejected System: real OS/network not required to prove non-blocking response behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Start handler with downstream processor stub that blocks (e.g., unresolved promise). Send valid request and assert response completes promptly while processor remains unfinished, then later allow processor completion.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a real-time concurrency SLO under load. Mocked timing or purely in-process stubs can hide scheduler/socket/backpressure effects; real HTTP + wall-clock behavior is the failure boundary. Rejected Integration: behavior explicitly requires wall-clock latency under concurrent slow work, which depends on runtime/OS/network stack effects.
behaviors.test_description: Run the service process with real HTTP requests; create concurrent slow downstream work from earlier events, then send new requests and measure end-to-end response time. Assert p95/each tested response <= 3s in this scenario.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Dedup correctness is at state handoff between handler and persistent queue/idempotency store. Unit logic alone can miss persistence/wiring mistakes. Rejected System: behavior text does not require external networked infra; local persistent test backend is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Use handler + real dedupe/persistence module (local DB/filesystem-backed test instance). Send same event ID twice and assert first is persisted, second is rejected/skipped with no second queue record.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: This is translation of queue-capacity signal into HTTP contract across module boundary. Unit tests of status mapping helper are too low because failure is often in wiring/error propagation. Rejected System: no need for real OS/network/subprocess to trigger and verify mapping.
behaviors.test_description: Run handler in-process with queue adapter configured to return/throw `QueueFull`. Send request and assert HTTP 503 plus `Retry-After` header value per contract; verify no downstream processing kick-off.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: The target is queue implementation + persistence/config wiring (not just mocked interface). Needs real queue module with local persistent backend to catch true enforcement bugs. Rejected System: behavior does not explicitly require external API or subprocess-based queue; local DB/filesystem-backed persistence fits Integration.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate the real persistent queue component against a test persistence backend, set capacity N, enqueue N events successfully, then enqueue N+1 and assert explicit capacity rejection and no extra persisted record.
